name: win64c-guard-caller-cluster

findings.summary: Atomically transitions the init counter at 0x140C36004 from -1 to 0 and initializes a stride-0x28 critical-section table.
evidence: Static qword at 0x140C36000 is 0xFFFFFFFF00000001, so the dword at 0x140C36004 starts as 0xFFFFFFFF.
The first instruction cluster performs LOCK INC on 0x140C36004 and only enters the initializer loop when the result is zero.
The initializer loop walks 0x140C559C0 toward 0x140C55B00 in 0x28-byte increments and calls 0x1407B7BA8 for each slot.
0x1407B7BA8 resolves to InitializeCriticalSectionEx with EDX=0xFA0 and R8D=0.
findings.inferred_semantics: Initialize an 8-slot CRITICAL_SECTION-style table once, using the -1-to-0 transition as the first-run gate.

findings.summary: Stores the requested guard index in the caller-provided object and enters the selected critical section.
evidence: Writes EDX into [RCX] before branching.
EDX==0 calls 0x1407D3714, which enters fallback critical section 0x140C56870.
For 1 <= EDX < 8, computes 0x140C559C0 + EDX*0x28 and calls 0x1407B7BB8.
0x1407B7BB8 resolves to KERNEL32.dll!EnterCriticalSection.

findings.summary: Atomically decrements the init counter and deletes the critical-section table when transitioning back below zero.
evidence: Uses EAX=-1 and LOCK XADD [0x140C36004], EAX.
Only enters the deletion loop when the old counter value was below 1.
The deletion loop walks 0x140C559C0 toward 0x140C55B00 in 0x28-byte increments and calls 0x1407B7BA0.
0x1407B7BA0 resolves to KERNEL32.dll!DeleteCriticalSection.
findings.inferred_semantics: Teardown mirror of 0x1407B62EC for the same 8-slot CRITICAL_SECTION-style table.

findings.summary: Reads the guard index from [RCX] and leaves the corresponding critical section.
evidence: Loads EAX from [RCX] and branches on zero/out-of-range values.
Index zero tail-jumps to 0x1407D3730, which leaves fallback critical section 0x140C56870.
For 1 <= EAX < 8, computes 0x140C559C0 + EAX*0x28 and calls 0x1407B7BC0.
0x1407B7BC0 resolves to KERNEL32.dll!LeaveCriticalSection.

findings.summary: Index-zero guard paths use a separate virtual-only critical-section object at 0x140C56870.
evidence: 0x1407D3714 calls 0x1407DC3E8, loads RCX with 0x140C56870, then tail-jumps through 0x140802480 (EnterCriticalSection).
0x1407D3730 loads RCX with 0x140C56870, then tail-jumps through 0x140802478 (LeaveCriticalSection).
Address-ref scan finds two direct LEA references to 0x140C56870; qword readback confirms the object is virtual-only data.

findings.summary: The previous callback registration thunk's indirect import is KERNEL32.dll!EncodePointer.
evidence: The IAT slot 0x140802648 resolves to KERNEL32.dll!EncodePointer.
This tightens the prior interpretation of 0x1407B7B64: it encodes the callback pointer before storing it into the runtime callback array.

tooling_updates.summary: Added a PE import-table reader and CLI command that reports DLL descriptors, function names/ordinals, and IAT entry VAs.
tooling_updates.client_case: Resolved guard/locale IAT slots such as 0x140802488 -> InitializeCriticalSectionEx and 0x140802648 -> EncodePointer.

tooling_updates.summary: Indirect RIP-memory calls now annotate IAT-backed callsites with resolved import metadata.
tooling_updates.client_case: Future function-call artifacts can carry import display names when a callsite targets an IAT slot.

tooling_updates.summary: Tightened LOCK-prefix scanning so false middle-of-instruction hits such as rip-relative-lea-lock are not reported.
tooling_updates.client_case: Regenerated guard caller address refs no longer include the prior false 0x1407B6C1F lea-lock hit.

expected_qword_warnings: 0x140C559C0 requested past file-backed .data into virtual zeros

next_worklist: Map 0x1407DC3E8 to identify how fallback critical section 0x140C56870 is initialized.
Propagate import-name annotations into pe-instructions branch/JMP payloads so tail thunks show imported API names inline.
Use the named critical-section wrappers to label downstream object lifetime and network/bootstrap paths that acquire guard indices.
